nativeRange.habitat: Clear seaward reefs and inner/outer reef crests near drop-offs and slopes; usually in mid-water above the substrate; depth range 0-75 m, typically 0-20 m

diet.notes: In the wild, primarily grazes on calcareous algae and consumes zooplankton from mid-water, with some phytoplankton; one of the more planktivorous triggerfishes. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet of meaty foods (squid, krill, clams, chopped shrimp, small fish, mysis) supplemented with marine pellets and vegetable matter such as nori or spirulina. Hard-shelled foods help wear down continuously growing teeth. LiveAquaria classifies it as carnivorous in a captive-care context.

goodWith: other large semi-aggressive to aggressive fish

summary: A hardy, mid-water triggerfish with a dark body and pale lines at the dorsal and anal fin bases. Aggressive and not reef safe, but one of the more planktivorous Balistidae.
careNotes:
The Black Triggerfish (often sold as Black Durgon or Hawaiian Black Triggerfish) is one of the hardier and more forgiving triggerfishes and is commonly considered suitable for less-experienced saltwater keepers willing to provide a large tank. Adults have been reported at 25-40 cm; FishBase lists a maximum of 50 cm TL, though aquarium specimens are generally smaller. Coloration appears black at a distance but iridesces blue-green to purplish under aquarium lighting, with thin pale lines at the base of the dorsal and anal fins.

It is generally advised to provide a tank of at least 500 L (about 130-180 US gal) with a long footprint, plentiful live rock arranged into caves and overhangs, and substantial open swimming space. Unlike most triggers, Black Durgons spend much of their time hovering in mid-water foraging on zooplankton, so vigorous flow and vertical swimming room matter as much as floor area. They have been known to rearrange rocks and rockwork.

Not reef safe: have been reported to nip corals and will generally consume shrimp, snails, crabs and other small invertebrates. Best kept as a single individual; conspecific aggression is high and pairs rarely cohabit outside very large systems. Compatible with other robust, similarly sized semi-aggressive to aggressive tankmates.

Cross-source values for adult size and temperature were averaged: LiveAquaria states a maximum of 35.6 cm and an aquarium range of 22.2-25.6 degrees C; FishBase lists 50 cm TL and a wild range of 24.4-28.9 degrees C; ReefGuide reports 15-35 cm. The minimum-tank recommendation was biased to the larger end (LiveAquaria 130 gal, multiple specialist sources 125-180 gal) and rounded to 500 L given the species' active mid-water swimming habit.
breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria. In the wild, the species is oviparous with distinct pairing during spawning; eggs are deposited in benthic nests and guarded.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary navigation root was https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/triggerfish; the species page is the Hawaiian Black Triggerfish listing. Note that LiveAquaria's separate 'Niger Triggerfish' listing is a different species (Odonus niger), not Melichthys niger.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Balistidae / Tetraodontiformes), circumtropical distribution, wild depth range (0-75 m), wild temperature range (24.4-28.9 C), maximum length (50 cm TL), and feeding biology (calcareous algae + zooplankton). Wild temperature range is slightly higher than LiveAquaria's aquarium recommendation; averaged 24-27 C as the captive target.

additional.name: ReefGuide.org
additional.notes: Cross-check for adult size (reports 15-35 cm) and circumtropical reef distribution including Caribbean records.

additional.name: Bay Bridge Aquarium / Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Additional hobby cross-check for minimum tank size (125 gal / ~473 L), lifespan (10-15 years in captivity), water flow needs, and confirmation that the species is not reef safe.

fish.breedingNotes: Not bred in captivity. Wild spawning is oviparous with distinct pair-bonding behavior.
